decision_summary: The design is feasible with a chip temperature of 58.6°C, well below the 85°C limit. However, the optimization history indicates diminishing returns from geometry changes and suggests a shift to material tuning. The heat spreader conductivity is currently at its upper bound (500.0), so no further increase is possible there. The next most effective lever is the base plate conductivity, which is currently low (24.0) compared to the spreader. Increasing it will improve vertical heat extraction towards the cooled boundaries.

changes.reason: Doubling the base plate conductivity within the allowed 2x step ratio to enhance heat transfer from the spreader to the side boundaries, addressing the strategy suggestion to prefer material tuning when geometry limits are reached.

expected_effects: Improved thermal gradient across the base plate
More efficient heat removal to the Dirichlet boundaries
risk_notes: Diminishing returns may occur if the bottleneck shifts entirely to the interface resistance or lateral spreading capacity
Material cost or weight implications in a real-world scenario (not simulated here)
